ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Pho Bo Dac Biet; closest parent 'beef noodle soup food product' (FOODON_03311737) used as the nearest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Pho Bo Dac Biet; A0FKP (beef noodle soup) used as closest parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code exists for this specific Vietnamese noodle soup preparation.

main_ingredients: fresh herbs (Thai basil, coriander, sawtooth herb)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant bowl of Pho Bo Dac Biet weighs approximately 600 g, comprising broth (~350 g), rice noodles (~120 g), assorted beef cuts (~100 g), and garnishes (~30 g).

nutrient_source.title: Pho Bo (Vietnamese Beef Noodle Soup) — composite nutrient estimate based on USDA FoodData Central entries for beef broth, cooked rice noodles, beef brisket, and beef tendon, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Vietnamese noodle soups
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods) and FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Pho Bo Dac Biet as a composite dish. Nutrient values per 100 g are estimated from weighted-average composition of the dish components: beef bone broth (~58% by weight), rice noodles (~20%), mixed beef cuts including brisket, tendon, tripe, and meatballs (~17%), and fresh garnishes (~5%). USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries used: beef broth (FDC ID 171155), cooked rice noodles (FDC ID 168930), beef brisket braised (FDC ID 174032), beef tendon cooked (FDC ID 172544). FAO/INFOODS SEA table consulted for regional calibration. Values represent a typical restaurant-prepared bowl and carry medium confidence.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — rice noodles and noodle soup analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Pho Bo Dac Biet. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on the closest analogous food: rice noodle soup (GI ~40–50) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables. The broth-dominant, mixed-protein composition of pho further attenuates glycemic response relative to plain rice noodles (GI ~53). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 34.8) / 100 = 15.7 g, where carbohydrate per 600 g serving = 5.8 × 6 = 34.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from bone broth and fish sauce
hypertension.guidance: A single 600 g bowl of Pho Bo Dac Biet provides approximately 1920 mg sodium, representing 83–96% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg, making it a high-concern food for individuals managing hypertension who should request reduced-sodium broth or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice noodles, partially offset by protein and broth volume
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 45 and a glycemic load of ~15.7 per bowl, Pho Bo Dac Biet has a moderate glycemic impact; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size of noodles, consider requesting extra protein and reduced noodle quantity, and pair with high-fibre garnishes such as bean sprouts and herbs to further blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from mixed beef cuts and bone broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (~1920 mg per bowl) and moderate saturated fat (~4.2 g per bowl) from beef tendon, brisket, and bone marrow broth are relevant concerns for cardiovascular disease management; individuals should limit frequency of consumption, choose leaner beef cuts where available, and avoid adding hoisin or chilli sauces high in sodium and sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pho Bo Dac Biet (Special Beef Noodle Soup) is the premium version of Vietnam's iconic pho bo, distinguished by its inclusion of multiple beef components in a single bowl. A typical 'dac biet' (special) serving contains rare sliced beef (tai), well-done brisket (chin), beef tendon (gan), honeycomb tripe (sach), and beef meatballs (bo vien), all served in a deeply aromatic bone broth that has been simmered for 6–12 hours with charred ginger, charred onion, star anise, cinnamon, cloves, cardamom, and coriander seeds. The broth is seasoned with fish sauce and rock sugar, producing a complex, savoury-sweet flavour profile with warm spice notes. The dish is served over flat rice noodles (banh pho) and garnished with fresh Thai basil, sawtooth herb, bean sprouts, lime wedges, and sliced chilli. In Singapore, this dish is served at Vietnamese restaurants and represents an authentic representation of Northern and Southern Vietnamese pho traditions. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy, high-protein meal with significant sodium content primarily from the broth and fish sauce. The rice noodles contribute a moderate glycemic load, partially attenuated by the high protein and liquid volume. The dish is dairy-free and rich in B vitamins, iron, and zinc from the beef components. Clinically, the primary concern is sodium content, which can approach or exceed daily recommended limits in a single serving, making it a high-concern food for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. For Type 2 Diabetes, the moderate glycemic index of rice noodles and the protein-rich composition make this a manageable choice with appropriate portion control.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Pho Bo Dac Biet as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted-average composition of dish components using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries and FAO/INFOODS SEA regional calibration. GI is an analogous estimate based on rice noodle soup data from the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation methodology and restaurant-level variability in broth concentration, noodle quantity, and beef cut proportions.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are per 100 g of the assembled dish as served (broth + noodles + beef + garnishes combined). Serving size estimated at 600 g for a standard restaurant bowl. 2. Sodium content is highly variable across restaurants; the estimate of 320 mg/100 g (1920 mg/bowl) reflects a typical restaurant preparation and may be higher in richer broths. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entries used for this composite estimate. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entries used for this composite estimate. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported with sufficient confidence across the component entries used. 6. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central component data for the beef and broth ingredients used. 7. The dish is served at Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant on Circular Road, Singapore, as identified in the GrabFood source record. 8. Halal status is listed as false as this cannot be confirmed without restaurant-level certification; the restaurant should be consulted directly. 9. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Pho Bo Dac Biet was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date.
